merged_events.event_description: A U.S. Consulate opened in Carlsruhe (Karlsruhe), the capital of Baden, establishing the first U.S. diplomatic presence in the capital.

evidence.quote: Carlsruhe (Karlsruhe), which opened on March 3, 1855 and closed on February 18, 1872

merged_events.event_description: George Bancroft was specially accredited as U.S. Minister to the Grand Duke of Baden, while also accredited to Prussia and the North German Confederation, establishing nonresident envoy relations with Baden.

evidence.quote: On July 19, 1868, specially accredited U.S. Minister to the Grand Duke of Baden George Bancroft and the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Grand Duchy of Baden Rudolph von Freydorf signed a treaty of naturalization between the United States and the Grand Duchy of Baden. At the time, Bancroft was also accredited to Prussia and the North German Confederation and, after 1871, to the German Empire.

merged_events.event_description: All diplomatic relations between the U.S. and the German Empire, including Baden as a constituent state, were severed when President Wilson instructed the Secretary of State to notify the German Ambassador.

evidence.quote: On February 3, 1917, U.S. President Woodrow Wilson instructed the Secretary of State to notify the German Ambassador to the United States that all diplomatic relations between the U.S. and the German Empire were severed. As the foreign affairs of Imperial Germany were run out of Berlin and decided upon by the Kaiser, this constituted the severance of relations with Baden, as part of the German Empire.

matched.notes: Perfect match on both date (1917-02-03) and status (None). Severance of diplomatic relations with the German Empire.

missing_from_csv.event_description: George Bancroft was specially accredited as U.S. Minister to the Grand Duke of Baden while also accredited to Prussia and the North German Confederation, establishing nonresident envoy relations with Baden.
missing_from_csv.notes: RDCR clearly documents this as a distinct diplomatic status change — Bancroft was specially accredited to Baden on this date. This represents an upgrade from Consulate to Envoy Nonresident status and should be added to the CSV between the Consulate and None rows.

discrepancies.reasoning: The CSV records the first Consulate as opening on 1832-07-13, while the extracted event (from RDCR) records a Consulate opening in Carlsruhe (Karlsruhe) on 1855-03-03. The 23-year gap is substantial. It is possible that the 1832 date refers to an earlier consulate in a different city within Baden (e.g., Mannheim), while the 1855 date refers specifically to the consulate in the capital Carlsruhe. The RDCR text explicitly says the Carlsruhe consulate 'opened on March 3, 1855,' but the CSV may be drawing on other sources (such as consular appointment records) for an earlier consular presence in Baden. Without additional source material, it is ambiguous which date correctly represents the first U.S. diplomatic presence in Baden.
